Q1: I organized my study routine around hands-on practice, honestly. I'd block out time on weekends mostly, maybe three or four hours at a stretch, where I could really dig into the material physically - writing things out, working through problems step by step. During the week it was harder because I was juggling work and other commitments, so I'd squeeze in shorter sessions when I could. I found that actually doing things, not just reading, helped me retain information better.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks things would pile up and I'd have to cram more toward deadlines. Being 26 and having responsibilities outside of just being a student means I can't always stick to a perfect schedule. But I tried to at least touch the material a few times each week, even if the duration changed.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check - new announcements, discussion posts from my peers, or to see if there was feedback on something I submitted. The peer support aspect really kept me coming back because I genuinely enjoyed seeing what others were discussing. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course and my classmates, especially since I don't have internet at home and had to use campus or library computers.
Q4: Yeah, checking the discussion forums became kind of a habit for me. I'd go to the library, get on a computer, and the first thing I'd do is check what my peers had posted. It started because I found real value in those conversations - people explaining concepts in different ways than the lectures. After a while it just became automatic, like checking messages. The peer interactions made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like a community space.
Q5: The discussion forums and video content were gold for me. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so watching someone demonstrate something and then being able to discuss it with peers really cemented the learning. I watched almost five hours of video content total, and I'd often pause and try things out myself. The peer interactions - I had over 40 of them - helped me see different perspectives and catch things I'd missed. That combination of watching, doing, and discussing worked really well for my learning style.
Q6: Some of the reading-heavy PDFs I'll admit I didn't engage with as much as I should have. When it's just walls of text without interactive elements, I struggle to stay focused. I'd skim them or sometimes skip to the summary sections. It's not that they weren't valuable, but my brain just works better when I can do something with the information rather than passively absorb it. I know my assignment completion rate reflects this - I prioritized the hands-on stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to something practical or when it involved collaboration with others. If I could apply what I was learning immediately or discuss it with peers, I was all in. The discussions where people shared real examples or struggled through problems together - those felt incredibly valuable. Activities that felt like busywork or just checking boxes, those were harder to motivate myself for.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which actually made me more likely to engage with some of them. The required stuff sometimes felt like pressure, especially when deadlines were tight. But optional discussions or extra resources? I could approach those with curiosity instead of stress. I probably engaged more deeply with optional peer discussions than some required readings, honestly, because I was choosing to be there.
Q9: Absolutely. Since I don't have internet at home, I'd download everything I could when I was on campus - videos, PDFs, lecture notes. Then I'd study from those materials at home, writing out notes by hand, working through problems on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with printed materials spread across my kitchen table. I'd also use textbooks from the library and sometimes YouTube tutorials I'd download when I had access.
Q10: Most of my deep studying happened at home with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper, making diagrams, physically organizing concepts with index cards. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. My 18 hours of weekly study time - probably only a third of that was actually online. The rest was me at my apartment, no internet, just grinding through the material the old-fashioned way.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video once, download it if possible, and then watch it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides and readings - one download, multiple study sessions. My actual engagement with that material was way higher than the single access would suggest. I'd return to the same downloaded PDF five or six times over a week, adding highlights and notes each time. The LMS only saw me grab it once.
Q12: There were a few weeks where work got really demanding and I fell behind. Not having internet at home meant I couldn't just quickly catch up in the evenings - I had to plan trips to campus or the library. That friction sometimes made it harder to stay on top of things. But honestly, my stress stayed pretty low overall because I'd learned to accept that I couldn't control everything. The peer support really helped during those tougher stretches.
Q13: Definitely. When work was crazy or I was tired, I'd prioritize the activities I could do offline over logging into the LMS. Fatigue especially affected my online engagement because getting to a computer required more effort than just studying at home. I'd sometimes batch my LMS activities - save up questions and posts for when I could get online rather than spreading them out. The competing responsibilities meant I had to be strategic about when I used the platform.
Q14: Sometimes I'd be on the LMS just going through the motions - clicking through materials, technically present but not really absorbing anything. This usually happened when I was tired or rushed between other obligations. I'd open a reading, scroll through it to mark it as viewed, but I knew I'd need to actually engage with it later offline when I could focus. Those sessions were more about staying connected than deep learning.
Q15: Yeah, I postponed some assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was prioritizing - I'd focus on the things I knew I could do well and delay the ones that felt overwhelming. Sometimes the readings felt so dense that I'd put them off hoping I'd have more energy later. My assignment completion rate wasn't great, I know. But I tried to make sure what I did submit was quality work, even if I couldn't get to everything.
Q16: Deadlines definitely drove a lot of my LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, checking requirements, posting in discussions to meet participation expectations. Without deadlines, I might have spread my engagement more evenly, but realistically they kept me accountable. I learned to work backward from deadlines to plan my limited online time. The structure helped, even if it meant my activity was sometimes clustered.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I got comments on my work, I'd really sit with them and try to improve. Positive feedback boosted my motivation to engage more, and constructive criticism helped me focus my efforts better. I appreciated when instructors or peers took time to give specific feedback - it made me feel like my contributions mattered. That definitely influenced how much effort I put into subsequent activities.
Q18: The course structure with regular discussion requirements actually worked well for me because it built in reasons to connect with peers. Having a rhythm of activities helped me plan around my limited internet access. The peer support intervention was perfect for my situation - it gave me a community even when I couldn't be online constantly. The structure provided scaffolding that I needed given my circumstances.
Q19: The discussion-based components made engagement easier because they played to my strengths in peer interaction. What made it harder was when activities required frequent online check-ins or assumed constant internet access. Timed activities were stressful because I couldn't always get to a computer when I needed to. But overall, the course design accommodated different learning approaches reasonably well, and I appreciated that flexibility.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce output that meets the requirements. They might be exploring, reading, watching videos, participating in discussions - all valuable activities - but then hit a wall when it comes to formal submissions. Life circumstances, perfectionism, or just not fully understanding expectations could all play into that gap between activity and submitted work.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance percentage was really low, but my exam score was decent and I got a Merit. A lot of my learning happened offline where it couldn't be tracked. Students who are resourceful, who download materials and study independently, who have good study strategies even if they're not constantly logged in - they can still perform well. The LMS activity is just one piece of the puzzle.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, how much time you spend. That's useful for seeing general engagement trends. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the thinking that happens away from the screen. They can't capture whether someone really understood a concept or just scrolled past it. And for students like me without home internet, they dramatically underrepresent actual study time.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, not just passively consuming it. It means I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to my own experience. For me, it also means being part of a learning community - discussing with peers, helping others understand, learning from their perspectives. Real engagement is when the course content occupies space in my mind even when I'm not officially studying.
Q24: The LMS data would miss how much I studied offline, all those hours with downloaded materials at my kitchen table. It wouldn't show the conversations I had with classmates in person about course content, or how I'd explain concepts to friends to test my own understanding. It can't capture that I was genuinely happy and motivated throughout the course, or that the peer support intervention made me feel connected even with my limited online access. The human elements of my learning experience are invisible to the data.
